critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Spring-edge regulars now working fresh green lanes like they’ve got the property mapped better than we do.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer use their white tail as a warning signal to alert others of danger nearby.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer are showing up along trail edges, food lines, openings, and fresh-cut green spots where the newest growth is easiest to grab.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Spring heavyweight now roaming harder and treating the woods like a slow-moving calorie treasure hunt.
critters.fact: Black bears have excellent memories and can remember food locations for years.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bear movement is more realistic around swamps, low ground, quiet timber, and natural feeding pockets where spring food stacks up.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Lean edge-country operator cruising spring routes like it punched in before sunrise.
critters.fact: Coyotes use varied vocalizations to communicate, including howls, yips, and barks for different social signals.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes are most likely working low-light lanes, two-tracks, trail crossings, and open edges where movement is easy to track.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Quick little field-edge hunter listening to the grass like it owes him money.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their excellent hearing to locate small animals burrowed underground before pouncing.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red foxes may be slipping across openings, pausing in grass, and locking onto tiny movement around brushy edges.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-loving woodland ghost keeping things quiet while the louder critters embarrass themselves in public.
critters.fact: Gray foxes are one of the few canids that can climb trees to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray foxes are more likely moving through thickets and wooded edges than strolling out where cameras get an easy look.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Silent spring predator slipping through cover like the woods forgot to make noise around it.
critters.fact: Bobcats can leap up to 10 feet vertically to catch prey or escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may be working transition zones where rabbits, squirrels, and ground birds are moving more often.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-moving bark goblin still browsing like time is fake and urgency is for squirrels.
critters.fact: Porcupines have sharp quills that detach easily to protect them from predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, porcupines may still be found near woody browse, tree trunks, and quiet cover where nobody is asking them to hurry.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Water engineer fully back on spring maintenance like the creek filed a work order.
critters.fact: Beavers are natural architects, building dams that create wetlands benefiting many other animals.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fresh chew marks, muddy slides, peeled sticks, and patched flow spots are good signs the beaver crew is active.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Wetland commuter now easier to catch in the open as shoreline traffic picks up.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges from vegetation, providing shelter and protection in wetlands and ponds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may be swimming shoreline routes, feeding near cattails, or popping through shallow water like tiny pond submarines.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Sharp little creek-side menace checking every muddy edge for something foolish enough to move.
critters.fact: Minks are excellent swimmers, using their webbed feet to catch fish and evade predators in water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink activity is most likely along creek banks, pond edges, muddy tracks, and narrow shoreline travel lanes.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Deep-woods prowler moving through timber like it read the map and ate the compass.
critters.fact: Fishers are skilled climbers and can hunt porcupines by flipping them over to reach unprotected areas.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fishers may be moving unseen through heavier timber and forest edges where tracks or trail cams tell the story first.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Water-loving troublemaker turning open channels and muddy banks into a personal playground.
critters.fact: River otters can hold their breath underwater for up to eight minutes while hunting fish.
critters.activeFact: Right now, otters may be cruising channels, sliding banks, and working ponds or creeks where fish and frogs are waking up.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Treetop busybody juggling nest work, feeder raids, and branch drama before breakfast.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels can find buried nuts using spatial memory, not just smell.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels are easy to spot racing branches, hauling material, raiding feeders, and acting personally offended by each other.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny woodland alarm system screaming from the trees like it owns every cone, branch, and bad idea.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels defend territories fiercely and store thousands of conifer cones for winter food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red squirrels are loud, twitchy, and fully prepared to yell at anything that crosses their imaginary property line.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Pocket-sized ground rocket back in full circulation and moving like spring came with espresso.
critters.fact: Chipmunks have cheek pouches to carry food back to their burrows for storage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks are darting around logs, rocks, burrows, wood piles, and trail edges like the woods installed tiny traffic lights.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Northern hare stuck in spring’s awkward costume change while the forest background keeps shifting.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color seasonally for camouflage, from brown in summer to white in winter.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snowshoe hares can be easier to notice where thinning cover and leftover pale fur do them no favors.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Brush-edge hopper feeding hard but never straying far from an emergency exit.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails use multiple forms of communication, including scent marking and thumping their hind feet.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails are most likely along yard edges, trail borders, brush piles, and sunny openings with fresh growth.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Night-shift bandit treating spring camp like somebody forgot to lock the snack cabinet.
critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws that can identify objects even underwater without using their eyes.
critters.activeFact: Right now, raccoons may be hitting pond edges, creeks, feeders, campsites, and anything that smells remotely negotiable.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Cold-sensitive night wanderer finally getting evenings that don’t feel like a personal attack.
critters.fact: Opossums have more teeth than any other North American land mammal—up to 50 teeth!
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may be showing up around brush, sheds, wood piles, water edges, and camp margins after dark.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Striped night forager roaming damp ground with one warning label and no need for backup.
critters.fact: Striped skunks spray a strong-smelling liquid as a defense mechanism to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, skunks may be working lawns, trails, roadsides, field edges, and soft soil where the digging is good.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: After-hours glider running silent tree traffic while the daylight squirrels hog the drama.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels can glide up to 150 feet using a special membrane between their limbs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they’re mostly unseen unless feeders, nest boxes, cavities, or nighttime cameras catch the quiet little air raids.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Small dusk hunter getting back to business whenever the bugs and temperature finally cooperate.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects, consuming up to half their body weight nightly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, warm dusk windows may bring little brown bats over open lanes, ponds, and edges for quick feeding loops.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tougher early-season bat willing to clock in on evenings the smaller crowd might still complain about.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats use echolocation to catch insects with amazing precision during nighttime flights.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mild evenings near buildings, lights, tree lines, and sheltered openings can bring big brown bats into view.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Deep-voiced woods boss still calling through spring nights like the timber belongs to him.
critters.fact: Barred Owls have distinctive hooting calls that sound like "Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?"
critters.activeFact: Right now, barred owl calls can carry clean through damp woods at dusk, dawn, and the quiet hours when everything else shuts up.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Early-nesting powerhouse already deep into family duty while half the woods is still flirting.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls have powerful talons that can crush prey as large as skunks and porcupines.
critters.activeFact: Right now, great horned owls are likely focused on hunting, guarding nest areas, and feeding young instead of making much romance noise.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Loud blue security guard acting like every feeder, tree, and suspicious shadow reports directly to him.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic the calls of hawks to scare away other birds from feeders.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays are loud, bossy, and quick to chase anything that lingers too long near their favorite trees.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Red spring singer posted up like sunrise hired him as the morning announcer.
critters.fact: Northern Cardinals are known for their bright red color and distinctive crest on their heads.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cardinals are strong morning voices around brush, yards, feeders, and woodland edges where pairs are locked in.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny feathered inspection crew checking cavities like the housing market just opened.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees recognize and remember hundreds of individual faces for months.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chickadees may be bouncing between feeders, branches, and potential nest holes with very serious tiny-bird business.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Curious gray songbird yelling from the branches like it has updates nobody requested.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice use a wide variety of calls to communicate with each other in forests and backyards.
critters.activeFact: Right now, titmice may be calling from exposed branches, checking holes, and moving through edge trees with spring confidence.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Upside-down bark specialist running tree trunks like gravity is merely a suggestion.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches can climb down tree trunks headfirst, unlike most other birds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nuthatches are busy on mature trunks, calling sharply, slipping headfirst down bark, and working old trees hard.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small drummer tapping out spring property claims one rapid burst at a time.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers drum on trees to communicate and establish territory during their lively forest performances.
critters.activeFact: Right now, quick sharp tapping on limbs or trunks often means a downy is broadcasting that this section is taken.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Bigger bark hammer sounding off from older timber like it brought the heavy tools.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers use their long tongues to extract insects from deep tree bark crevices.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hairy woodpeckers may be easier to hear than see, especially in taller trees and mature woodland edges.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Giant forest carpenter smashing into spring like subtlety was never invited.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers create large rectangular holes when foraging for insects in dead trees.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deep pounding and loud calls can reveal a pileated long before that red crest flashes through the trees.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Vocal woodland hammerhead turning spring competition into a full neighborhood argument.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers use their strong beaks to drum on trees for communication and finding food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may spend as much time scolding, calling, and defending space as they do feeding.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced nest minimalist proving once again that two twigs and optimism count as architecture.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly at speeds up to 55 miles per hour, making them swift and agile birds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, paired doves may be cooing, chasing, landing awkwardly, and pretending their nest plan is structurally sound.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Late-brightening little seed fan slowly swapping winter dullness for summer swagger.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice a year, changing bright yellow feathers to a duller olive color in winter.
critters.activeFact: Right now, goldfinches may still look halfway changed but are increasingly lively around feeders, weeds, and open edges.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-weather ground feeder still hanging around like it didn’t get spring’s eviction notice.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos are often called "snowbirds" because they appear in northern areas during winter months.
critters.activeFact: Right now, juncos may still be picking under feeders, though their winter dominance is clearly fading from the scene.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social little singer turning feeder visits into spring auditions.
critters.fact: House Finches are known for their cheerful, warbling songs that vary regionally across North America.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches are more vocal, more paired-up, and more obvious around feeders, brush, and nearby trees.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland visitor showing up in little waves like it enjoys keeping the schedule mysterious.
critters.fact: Purple Finches have a fruity, raspberry-like song often heard during early spring mornings.
critters.activeFact: Right now, purple finches can appear briefly, feed hard, and vanish again before anyone gets too confident.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Full strut-season chaos machine turning every clearing into a stage and every hen into an audience.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can fly up to 55 miles per hour for short distances to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gobblers may be strutting in fields, trails, sunny openings, and woodland pockets like they’re charging admission.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest drummer thumping from hidden logs like the woods grew its own bass speaker.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse create a drumming sound by rapidly beating their wings to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, that low thumping rhythm may be rolling through thicker cover more often as late-April display peaks.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall prehistoric-looking loudmouth bringing marsh drama with a voice that refuses to stay local.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform elaborate dances involving bowing, jumping, and wing-flapping to communicate and attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, their rattling calls and slow wingbeats are hard to miss across fields, wet spots, and open country.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Black-feathered property auditor checking every field, branch, and questionable situation.
critters.fact: American Crows use tools and can recognize individual human faces to avoid threats.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crows are loud at sunrise, nosy all day, and quick to investigate anything dead, shiny, edible, or suspicious.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Big old-soul corvid cruising over timber like it knows secrets the rest of the woods forgot.
critters.fact: Common Ravens use tools and can mimic sounds, including human speech, showcasing their intelligence.
critters.activeFact: Right now, ravens may be soaring in pairs, calling over forest blocks, and repeating familiar routes with serious purpose.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Big soaring raptor balancing nest duty with the cold stare of an airborne landlord.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks use soaring flight to conserve energy while searching large areas for prey.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red-tails may be circling, perching near nest sites, and watching open ground for careless movement.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland chaser threading trees like the forest forgot to install speed limits.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are agile hunters, often catching birds mid-flight using powerful surprise attacks.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sudden darting flights through trees may be a Cooper’s hawk patrolling its claim and rattling every songbird nearby.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Classic spring duck making use of every puddle, pond, ditch, and wet corner with confidence.
critters.fact: Mallard ducks can sleep with one eye open to stay alert for predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mallards may be loafing, feeding, or cruising shallow flooded spots that finally look useful after winter.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Flashy backwater specialist slipping into quiet corners like it paid extra for privacy.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities, making them one of the few ducks that perch and nest above water.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may be tucked into sheltered bends, pond edges, flooded woods, and quiet pockets where they can vanish fast.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Shoreline bodyguard fully convinced nesting season gives it legal authority over the pond.
critters.fact: Canada Geese have distinctive honking calls to communicate and stay together during long migratory flights.
critters.activeFact: Right now, geese around ponds and fields may be loud, stubborn, and already judging whether you’re too close.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Long-legged shoreline statue hunting with the patience of something that skipped coffee and still wins.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons can stand motionless for long periods, waiting to catch fish with lightning-fast strikes.
critters.activeFact: Right now, a heron standing motionless near a pond, creek, or marsh edge is spring doing its quiet predator thing.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Sun-powered pond regular stacking onto logs every time April offers a warm enough deal.
critters.fact: Painted turtles can absorb oxygen through their skin while underwater, helping them stay submerged longer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny logs, banks, and pond edges may start collecting turtles like little solar panels with legs.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Heavy aquatic tank slowly coming back online as the water finally stops feeling rude.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles have powerful jaws and can deliver a surprisingly strong bite to defend themselves.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snapping turtles may be shifting through shallows, muddy bottoms, and warming edges with that old swamp-monster energy.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Panfish edging toward shallow business like the pond calendar just flipped pages.
critters.fact: Bluegill can rapidly change their spawning colors to attract mates during breeding season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bluegill may be holding near wood, weeds, dark-bottom shallows, and protected edges that warm faster.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Shallow-water lurker staging with the patience of something waiting to ruin a minnow’s day.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can expand their stomachs to swallow prey nearly half their own body length.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may be sitting near weeds, wood, edges, and calmer shallows where the next seasonal move is brewing.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Striped little school fish moving through spring water with more purpose than winter ever allowed.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have distinctive vertical stripes and are popular freshwater fish for sport fishing in North America.
critters.activeFact: Right now, perch may be moving in groups through nearshore water, weeds, and softer shallows where conditions are improving.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Toothy spring missile built for flooded weeds, shallow ambush lanes, and bad news.
critters.fact: Northern Pike have sharp teeth and can strike prey with explosive speed in freshwater habitats.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may still be drawn to flooded edges, soft shallows, weeds, and slow water that looks harmless until it isn’t.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny frog with unreasonable volume turning wet nights into a full campground sound system.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers' loud peeping calls are often the first sounds of spring in North American wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, warm wet nights can set off peeper choruses in marshes, ditches, low spots, and pond edges like somebody flipped a switch.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big pond bass drum still waiting for warmer water before adding his opinion to the night shift.
critters.fact: American Bullfrogs can produce loud calls that carry over a mile to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs may still be quiet compared with peepers and leopard frogs, but warmer edges can start waking them.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline frog easing back into pond life while the louder spring crew steals the headlines.
critters.fact: Green frogs are known for their distinctive "gunk" call, often heard near ponds and wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, green frogs may be appearing around calm shallows, sunny banks, wet pockets, and pond edges with decent warmth.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted spring jumper taking advantage of wet grass, shallow pools, and any warm patch that behaves.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs have distinctive dark spots resembling a leopard’s coat, helping them camouflage in wetlands.
critters.activeFact: Right now, leopard frogs may show up near puddles, ditches, marsh edges, and wet grassy spots before the woods fully leaf out.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Sun-chasing ribbon of muscle popping out wherever warmth finally wins the argument.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can release a mild musk from their glands to deter predators when threatened.
critters.activeFact: Right now, garter snakes may be warming on trails, logs, rocks, leaf litter, garden edges, and sunny debris piles.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Bank-side bruiser slowly returning to shoreline duty as the water warms enough to be worth it.
critters.fact: Water snakes can hold their breath underwater for up to 30 minutes while hunting for prey.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny banks near calm water offer the best chance of seeing a water snake testing the late-April conditions.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive cover snake sliding back into the season like it prefers nobody announce it.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes mimic venomous snakes with their colorful patterns to avoid predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, milk snakes may turn up near wood piles, old boards, barns, brushy edges, and sheltered cover on mild afternoons.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Northbound orange traveler still more rumor than regular in late-April northern Michigan.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles between North America and Mexico each year.
critters.activeFact: Right now, monarchs are still more of an approaching-season sign than a daily local sight around camp.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Big-winged warm-weather showoff still waiting for spring to stop being moody.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies can taste with their feet to find suitable plants for laying eggs.
critters.activeFact: Right now, swallowtails are still likely sparse, with better activity coming as blooms and warm afternoons build.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Future sky hunter still mostly running the underwater level while April loads the next scene.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly up to 30 miles per hour and are excellent hunters, catching prey mid-air.
critters.activeFact: Right now, ponds may look quiet above the surface while young dragonflies are still growing below in the mud and vegetation.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Tiny wetland flyer still mostly hidden below the surface before the delicate summer patrol begins.
critters.fact: Damselflies fold their wings along their bodies when resting, unlike dragonflies that keep wings spread.
critters.activeFact: Right now, damselflies are more likely tucked into pond margins underwater than hovering over reeds and shoreline plants.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer light crew still waiting backstage while spring handles the opening acts.
critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescence to communicate, attracting mates with their unique light patterns in the dark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, the woods may feel alive again, but firefly nights are still further down the trail.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Early pollinator taking every sunny break like management finally approved overtime.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by performing a unique "waggle dance" to share food locations with hive mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, sunny windows may bring honeybees to early flowers, damp ground, and warm sheltered spots before they tuck back in.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Big fuzzy queen scout flying low like she’s shopping for flowers and a future empire.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can regulate their body temperature by vibrating muscles to stay warm in cold weather.
critters.activeFact: Right now, large bumblebees drifting low over grass, leaves, and edges are often queens scouting food and nesting ground.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Still mostly benched while spring lets frogs, bees, and wetland bugs steal the show.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers can hear through their front legs using special organs called tympana.
critters.activeFact: Right now, grasshoppers are still mostly waiting offstage while the season warms the ground and builds summer habitat.
